summary: This article documents a series of public conversations between Martin Fowler, Kent Beck, and David Heinemeier Hansson about whether TDD is 'dead.' Sparked by DHH's provocative RailsConf keynote, the discussions explore TDD's value, costs, and appropriate contexts. The participants find substantial common ground: all value self-testing code highly, agree TDD works well in some contexts, and emphasize that developers must thoughtfully adapt techniques to their situations rather than following dogma. The conversation serves as a 'reboot' to first principles after years of accumulated interpretations and misapplications of TDD practices.
key_points: TDD and self-testing code are often conflated but are distinct concepts - TDD is one way to achieve self-testing code, not the only way
Test-induced design damage often stems from excessive mocking and isolation, not from TDD itself - it's about design decisions, not the testing workflow
The value of testing involves trade-offs around frequency, fidelity, overhead, and lifespan - there's no universal right answer
Over-testing is a real concern: tests with zero delta coverage should be deleted, and the ratio of test code to production code is a bogus metric
Both TDD and the broader testing culture benefit from periodic 'reboots' to first principles after accumulating cruft and misinterpretations over time
money_quotes: Programmers deserve to feel confident that their code works, TDD is one (not the only) way to reach that.
Ascribing test-induced damage to TDD was like driving a car to a bad place and blaming the car for it.

You don't have enough tests (or good enough tests) if you can't confidently change the code, and the sign of too much is whenever you change the code you think you expend more effort changing the tests than changing the code.
The on-call is the feedback loop that teaches you what tests you didn't write.
Most people can't leave good ideas the fuck alone.
You either die a hero or become the villain.
TDD isn't dead, but is glad David set fire to it so it could come out like a phoenix.
If you're involved in software development you have to be thoughtful about it, you have to build up what practices work for you and your team, you can't take any technique blindly.
key_insight: TDD remains valuable but must be applied thoughtfully based on context - the real goal is self-testing code that gives developers confidence, and achieving that matters more than adherence to any particular workflow.
